figures.gloss: (c. 1832–1893) Rongowhakaata visionary deported without trial to Wharekauri (the Chatham Islands) in 1866 amid the East Coast wars. There, sick and imprisoned, he received the visions that founded the Ringatū faith; in July 1868 he seized the supply schooner Rifleman and sailed some three hundred whakarau (exiles) home. Hunted for four years after the reprisal killings at Matawhero (November 1868), sheltered by Tūhoe, pardoned in the 1883 amnesty, he spent his last decade as a man of peace, composing waiata and raising carved meeting houses.
figures.facet: Prophet, guerrilla, and liturgist in one span: the faith was forged in a prison camp, carried home by an act of piracy, and matured into pacifism — the founder's own arc from war to peace is the church's charter.
figures.provenance: Judith Binney, 'Redemption Songs: A Life of Te Kooti Arikirangi Te Turuki' (1995), the definitive study; William Greenwood, 'The Upraised Hand' (1942).

figures.gloss: The liturgy Te Kooti built entirely from the Māori Bible — psalms, prophets, and prayers memorized and recited, for decades deliberately unwritten — closed with the right hand raised to Ihowa, the gesture that names the faith. Worship gathers on the Twelfths, the twelfth of each month, with annual planting (huamata) and first-fruits (pure) rites binding the agricultural year into scripture; Māori suffering is read throughout as Israel's exile, the people as a covenant remnant awaiting return to their land.
figures.facet: A scripture-religion held almost wholly in MEMORY: the whakapapa-trained Māori art of exact oral recital applied to the Bible — the book internalized so completely it needed no book.
figures.provenance: Binney, 'Redemption Songs' (1995); Greenwood, 'The Upraised Hand' (1942); the Twelfths and the huamata/pure rites are documented Ringatū practice.

figures.gloss: (1873–1939) Whanganui farmer to whom, on 8 November 1918 — with the influenza pandemic killing Māori at several times the Pākehā rate — the Holy Spirit spoke, appointing him Māngai, the Mouthpiece of Ihoa o ngā Mano, Jehovah of Hosts. His healing mission drew thousands to Rātana Pā; the movement constituted itself as Te Haahi Rātana in 1925, then turned political: in April 1936 he sealed a covenant with Labour's Michael Joseph Savage with four symbolic gifts — a potato (the landless Māori), a broken gold watch (the broken machinery of the Treaty), a pounamu hei-tiki (the mana of the people), and a huia feather (the chiefly standing Savage would earn by mending the rest) — and by 1943 his nominees, the Four Quarters, held all four Māori parliamentary seats.
figures.facet: Where Te Kooti's church stayed liturgical, the Māngai's became a POLITICAL instrument in documented time — prophet-healer to kingmaker inside two decades, dispossession channeled into four seats that anchored Māori–Labour politics for half a century.
figures.provenance: J. McLeod Henderson, 'Ratana: The Man, the Church, the Political Movement' (1963; 2nd ed. 1972); Keith Newman, 'Ratana Revisited' (2006).

figures.gloss: The temple opened at Rātana Pā, near Whanganui, on 25 January 1928 — the Māngai's birthday — its twin domed towers named Arepa and Omeka, Alpha and Omega, crowned with the whetū mārama, the crescent-moon-and-star emblem of the church. The pā around it is the movement's capital and archive, and every January the nation's political leaders travel there for the anniversary gathering.
figures.facet: A prophetic movement that built its own capital and made the state visit it: the annual January pilgrimage of prime ministers to Rātana Pā re-enacts the 1936 covenant in reverse — the government comes to the prophet's village, not the prophet to the capital.
figures.provenance: Henderson (1963/1972); Newman, 'Ratana Revisited' (2006). The annual January gathering at Rātana Pā is standing New Zealand political practice.

edges.note: The founder and his liturgy: Te Kooti composed the Ringatū services from memorized Māori scripture in the Wharekauri prison camp, and the raised right hand of his practice became the faith's very name.
The Māngai and his holy village are one cult: the 1918 vision, the healing pilgrimages, and the 1928 Temple bind Rātana permanently to Rātana Pā.

Two waves of Māori prophetic response fifty years apart — Ringatū out of war and exile (1868), Rātana out of influenza and land loss (1918); separate churches, the same covenant-Israel reading of Māori suffering.

GOLD SEAM (substrate): the Māngai required followers to renounce tohunga practice and the old tapu — a Māori prophet dismantling the tapu system from within, even as his movement fought for Māori land and mana.

GOLD SEAM (substrate): Ringatū worship lives on the marae and in carved meeting houses Te Kooti himself raised — Binney documents his house-building, notably Te Tokanganui-a-noho at Te Kūiti (1873) — scripture recited inside the ancestor's body.

GOLD SEAM: Rātana liturgy invokes Te Matua, Te Tama, Te Wairua Tapu — and with them ngā Anahera Pono, the Faithful Angels, and the Māngai himself; the mainstream churches broke with the movement in the mid-1920s over precisely this extension of Trinitarian prayer.

GOLD SEAM: a church built wholly of Christian scripture in Māori, received from the missions and re-covenanted — yet with its centre of gravity in Ihowa and the prophets of Israel's exile rather than in the missionaries' own churches, which Te Kooti's people refused.

Prophets of dispossessed peoples inside settler states, visions turned into new liturgies in the same generation — Te Kooti's faith survived armed pursuit into a living church, where the Ghost Dance was broken at Wounded Knee.

Prophet-healers raised within three years of each other (Rātana 1918, Kimbangu 1921), each founding an independent indigenous church under a settler or colonial state — Rātana channelled his into parliament where Kimbangu's grew through its founder's imprisonment.
Each movement built its own holy capital — Rātana Pā with its twin-towered temple, Nkamba-Jerusalem with its mausoleum and pool — the prophet's home village promoted to centre of the religious world.

dossier.essence: The two great Māori prophetic churches of Aotearoa New Zealand: Ringatū, founded from the visions Te Kooti Arikirangi Te Tūruki received in 1867–68 as a prisoner on Wharekauri (the Chatham Islands), and Te Haahi Rātana, founded after Tahupōtiki Wiremu Rātana's vision of 8 November 1918 during the influenza pandemic. Both are Bible-centred faiths raised by Māori prophets in response to war, land loss, and epidemic, both read Māori suffering as Israel's exile, and both remain living, census-recorded denominations.
dossier.cosmos: Both churches are monotheist in the biblical frame, worshipping Ihowa (Jehovah; in Rātana usage Ihoa o ngā Mano, Jehovah of Hosts), with the Māori people understood as a covenant remnant awaiting restoration to their land. Rātana liturgy extends Trinitarian prayer to include ngā Anahera Pono, the Faithful Angels, and the Māngai (the Mouthpiece, Rātana himself) — the point on which the mission churches broke with the movement in the mid-1920s. The Māngai also required followers to renounce tohunga practice and the old tapu, dismantling the older Māori sacred system from within even as the movement fought for Māori land and mana.
dossier.practice: Ringatū worship gathers on the Twelfths — the twelfth of each month — for services of psalms, prophets, and prayers from the Māori Bible, memorized and recited with the right hand upraised to Ihowa (the gesture that names the faith), with annual planting (huamata) and first-fruits (pure) rites, held on marae and in carved meeting houses Te Kooti himself raised. The Rātana church, constituted in 1925, built its own capital at Rātana Pā near Whanganui, opening the twin-towered Temple of Ihoa in 1928 under the whetū mārama emblem, and turned prophetic authority political: the 1936 covenant with Labour's Michael Joseph Savage led to Rātana members holding all four Māori parliamentary seats by 1943, and the nation's political leaders still travel to Rātana Pā each January.
dossier.canon: The Paipera Tapu, the Māori Bible, is the substrate of both: Te Kooti composed the Ringatū liturgy entirely from it and it was held unwritten, in trained memory, for decades; the Rātana church likewise centres its own body of hymns and prayers on scripture addressed to Ihoa o ngā Mano.
dossier.reflects: Prophetic reappropriation — the colonized taking the colonizer's own scripture and re-covenanting it as their charter, so that religion becomes the surviving political instrument of a dispossessed people.
sources: Judith Binney, 'Redemption Songs: A Life of Te Kooti Arikirangi Te Turuki' (1995)
William Greenwood, 'The Upraised Hand: The Spiritual Significance of the Rise of the Ringatu Faith' (1942)
J. McLeod Henderson, 'Ratana: The Man, the Church, the Political Movement' (1963)
Judith Binney and Gillian Chaplin, 'Ngā Mōrehu: The Survivors' (1986)

links.cite: Judith Binney and Gillian Chaplin, 'Ngā Mōrehu: The Survivors' (1986)
